sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Citrus Australia, Australian state agriculture and biosecurity agencies, university horticulture extension, peer-reviewed research, and the Rare Fruit Council of Australia and WANATCA archives.

core.intro: Pomelo (also spelled pummelo, and once called the shaddock) is the largest of all the citrus and the parent the grapefruit was bred from. Across Australia the basics are the same: it wants full sun, free draining soil, a grafted named variety, and warmth, because pomelo is the most heat loving and the most frost tender of the common citrus. The state sections below cover the climate, the growing regions, harvest timing and the pests where you are. The notes here apply anywhere in the country.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Pomelos split by flesh colour. The red and pink fleshed types are the sweet, mild, eating pomelos: Carter's Red and Red Rouge carry deep pink to red flesh, and Chandler is a pink fleshed variety bred at the University of California from two Thai pomelos. The white, yellow and pale green fleshed types include Nam Roi, a popular seedless Vietnamese variety, Flicks Yellow, which is the most cold hardy of the group and a good choice for cooler districts, and the green fleshed Tahitian with its lime like overtones. Pomelo is the most heat loving citrus, so if you garden in a warm, frost free spot you can grow almost any variety, while cooler gardens lean on the hardier ones. Pomelo is sweeter and far less bitter than its descendant the grapefruit, so if you want a sharp citrus for the kitchen a lemon or lime will suit you better. The in stock table above shows the named varieties for sale right now and what they cost.

sections.heading: Pollination and seeds
sections.body: Whether a pomelo needs a second tree depends on the variety. Most of the named pomelos sold in Australia, including Nam Roi and Carter's Red, set a good crop on their own, but pomelo as a species is variable, and some traditional Asian varieties are self incompatible, meaning their own pollen will not set fruit and they crop poorly without a different citrus nearby. The flip side is seeds: a pomelo that is left to itself, or grown near pollen sterile citrus such as a navel orange, tends to be seedless or nearly so, while the same tree cross pollinated by other seedy citrus sets far more pips. So a lone Nam Roi gives you the seedless fruit it is prized for, whereas the same tree in a mixed citrus patch buzzing with bees will carry more seeds. If a named variety is listed as self fertile, one tree is enough.

sections.heading: Why pomelo trees are grafted
sections.body: Most pomelos sold here are grafted, with a named variety joined to a separate citrus rootstock, because a grafted tree fruits sooner and comes true to the variety on the label, while a seedling is slower to bear (often four to five years or more) and can vary from its parent. The rootstock also sets much of how the tree behaves, including its eventual size and how it copes with heavy, alkaline or disease prone soil. Pomelos are grafted onto rootstocks such as pomelo seedling, Cleopatra and King mandarin, rough lemon and Rangpur lime, and for a genuinely small tree onto the dwarfing Flying Dragon, which holds a tree to roughly half size and suits a large pot. In its Asian home the best pomelos are also propagated by air layering (marcotting), but for an Australian backyard a grafted nursery tree is the practical choice.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Pomelo wants full sun and free draining soil with a slightly acidic pH of about 6 to 6.5. It is the most frost tender of the common citrus, so this is the tree to give the warmest, most sheltered corner you have, and to shelter or cover through its first few winters in any district that gets frost. On heavy or wet ground plant on a low mound so water never sits around the roots, and always set the tree at the depth it grew in the nursery rather than burying the trunk or the graft union. Mulch well to feed the soil and hold moisture, but keep the mulch a hand width clear of the trunk to avoid collar rot. Container grown trees can go in through most of the year as long as you keep the water up over summer (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Pomelo is a thirsty, hungry tree like all citrus. Water matters most through flowering, fruit set and the long summer of fruit sizing, so water young and bearing trees deeply and regularly over the warm months and ease back in winter. Drainage counts as much as watering, because soggy soil invites the root and collar rot (Phytophthora) that kills citrus, so never let a tree stand in water. On feeding, citrus are heavy nitrogen users: build up from a light feed in the early years to several feeds a year on a bearing tree, splitting the year's nitrogen across late winter, spring and early summer rather than dumping it at once, and avoid feeding into late autumn, which pushes soft growth into the cold. Use a complete citrus fertiliser plus compost or well rotted manure. On the sandy and alkaline soils common in much of Australia, watch for the yellow leaves of zinc, iron and manganese deficiency, where these trace elements lock up, and correct it with a citrus trace element or chelated spray.

sections.heading: Harvest, ripening and eating
sections.body: Pomelo does not ripen any further once it is picked, so leave the fruit on the tree until it actually tastes sweet rather than picking on size or colour, because fruit taken too early is more acid and dry. Its great advantage is that it stores on the tree: a ripe pomelo hangs for months, slowly sweetening, so you can pick a few at a time. The fruit is huge, up to a couple of kilograms, with a thick pith that peels away easily, after which the segments skin by hand and the sweet, mild pulp lifts out cleanly with none of the bitterness of grapefruit. The thick rind candies well and gives the fruit a long shelf life. One thing to know before you eat a lot of it: like grapefruit, pomelo contains furanocoumarins that can interfere with how the body breaks down some prescription medicines, so if you take regular medication check with a pharmacist or doctor first. A grafted tree usually carries its first worthwhile crop within about two to four years.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted pomelo rather than an unnamed seedling. A seedling is slow to bear and may not match its parent, while a grafted named variety fruits within a few years and to type. Check the label for the variety and its flesh colour (red and pink types such as Carter's Red for sweet eating, pale and seedless types such as Nam Roi), and if the tree is bound for a pot or a small courtyard look for one grown on a dwarfing rootstock. Pomelo is also sold as part of a multi graft citrus tree, sharing one rootstock with a lemon, lime or grapefruit, which is a neat way to fit it into a small garden. The in stock table above lists the varieties available right now.

faqs.q: Is a pomelo just a big grapefruit?
faqs.a: It is the other way around: the pomelo came first and the grapefruit was bred from it, as a natural cross between pomelo and sweet orange. A pomelo is bigger, with thicker pith, and its flesh is sweeter and much less bitter, so where grapefruit can be sharp and you might sugar it, a good pomelo is mild enough to eat segment by segment like an easy peel orange.

faqs.q: Does pomelo interact with medications the way grapefruit does?
faqs.a: Yes. Pomelo contains the same furanocoumarin compounds as grapefruit, which can change how the body processes a number of common prescription medicines, and government medicines authorities list pomelo alongside grapefruit as a fruit that can interact. Being sweeter does not make it safer in this respect, so if you take regular medication, check with your pharmacist or doctor before eating pomelo often.

faqs.q: Can I grow a pomelo tree in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, as long as you start with a tree on a dwarfing rootstock such as Flying Dragon and give it a large container, a free draining mix and at least six hours of sun. Pots dry out and leach nutrients fast, so water whenever the top of the mix is dry and feed a little every month through the warmer half of the year. A pot also lets you move a frost tender pomelo under cover on cold nights, which is the only way many cool climate gardeners can fruit one.

faqs.q: How long until a pomelo fruits, and how big does the tree get?
faqs.a: A grafted tree usually starts bearing within about two to four years, while a seedling takes longer. Left alone a pomelo can become a large tree, from five up to fifteen metres in the ground, but it takes pruning well and is easily held to a height you can pick and net, and on a dwarfing rootstock in a pot it stays around two metres.

QLD.intro: Growing pomelo in Queensland. Queensland is the home of the Australian pomelo: it is the citrus best suited to warm, humid heat, and almost all of the named varieties in the nursery trade are grown and sold here, from the subtropical south east to the tropical far north.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pomelo is the most heat tolerant of the citrus and the most at home in Queensland's warmth and humidity, coping with the tropical and subtropical conditions far better than a frost shy lemon or a heat stressed apple ever would. That is why it is grown right up the state, and why Queensland nurseries carry the deepest range of pomelo varieties in the country. The one limit is cold: pomelo dislikes frost, so the cold pockets of the Granite Belt and the western Downs are its weak spot, while the warm coast and the tropical north suit it best.

sections.heading: Where pomelo grows in QLD
sections.body: Queensland is Australia's largest citrus producer by some measures and grows the widest range of pomelo. The traditional citrus heartland is the Central Burnett around Gayndah and Mundubbera, inland from Bundaberg, and pomelo is also grown in the warm Far North around the Atherton Tableland and Mareeba. Specialist subtropical nurseries through the south east, from the Sunshine Coast hinterland down toward the border, propagate most of the pomelo varieties sold across the country.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's warmth brings pomelo on early. The fruit matures through autumn and can then be held on the tree for some months, so a Queensland tree often gives ripe fruit from about autumn into winter, with the warmest northern districts leading the southern states. Taste a fruit before you strip the tree, because pomelo picked before it is sweet stays acid and dry.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland fruit fly is endemic here and will sting pomelo, so backyard fruit needs baiting or bagging. The native bronze orange bug (stink bug) sucks the spring shoots and flower stalks of citrus and is worst through spring and early summer, and citrus gall wasp, a Queensland native, raises woody galls on the stems. Queensland also carries the country's most serious citrus biosecurity story: Asiatic citrus canker was found at Emerald in 2004 and eradicated, and a separate 2018 outbreak in the Northern Territory and Western Australia was wiped out so that Australia was declared free of the disease in April 2021. It is why moving citrus plants and budwood between states is tightly controlled.

sections.heading: Why so many pomelos are sold Queensland only
sections.body: If you have shopped for pomelo online you will have seen varieties marked Queensland only. Citrus is a host of Queensland fruit fly and of diseases such as citrus canker, so live citrus plants and budwood are subject to interstate movement controls, and the fruit fly free states and Western Australia restrict or refuse incoming citrus. Many of the specialist Queensland nurseries that hold the best pomelo range therefore sell those trees for delivery within Queensland (and sometimes the eastern states) only, which is why local stock matters so much for this species.

faqs.q: Can I grow a pomelo on the humid Queensland coast where other citrus struggle?
faqs.a: Pomelo is the citrus most at home in humid tropical and subtropical heat, so the coast and the lowland tropics suit it better than they suit many other fruit trees. Give it full sun, free draining soil and good air movement, keep up the fruit fly control, and a coastal Queensland pomelo will usually crop well where a cool climate apple or cherry would fail outright.

faqs.q: Which pomelo variety is best for a Queensland backyard?
faqs.a: For sweet, mild eating the red and pink fleshed types such as Carter's Red are hard to beat, while the seedless Vietnamese Nam Roi is prized where you want fruit without pips. All do well in the Queensland warmth, so the choice comes down to flesh colour and whether you want seedless fruit; buy a grafted tree of a named variety rather than an unknown seedling.

WA.intro: Growing pomelo in Western Australia. Pomelo is the most heat loving citrus, so in WA it crops best on the warm Perth coastal plain and through the warm north, while the state's strict citrus quarantine makes locally grown trees especially valuable.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pomelo wants warmth and dislikes cold, which shapes where it grows in WA. The Mediterranean Perth coastal plain, with its hot dry summers, ripens pomelo well, and the warm subtropical Gascoyne and the tropical Ord at Kununurra are warmer still and frost free. The weak spots are the cooler, frost prone hill and inland districts, where a frost tender pomelo needs a sheltered, sun facing position and protection while young. As with all WA citrus, the deep coastal sand is often a bigger challenge than the climate.

sections.heading: Where pomelo grows in WA
sections.body: WA's citrus belt runs along the coast from Harvey south of Perth, up through Gingin and Moora, to Carnarvon on the central coast and Kununurra in the far north. The warm, frost free districts that best suit a pomelo are the Perth coastal plain, Gingin, the subtropical Gascoyne around Carnarvon, and tropical Kununurra on the Ord, while the cooler Perth Hills and Bindoon and Chittering districts inland are more frost prone and marginal for it.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: Pomelo matures from autumn and holds on the tree well into winter, so WA gives fruit over a long late season. The warm Gascoyne around Carnarvon ripens its citrus earliest, ahead of the cooler south west, so between the warm north and the Perth coast the state can pick pomelo across an extended autumn to winter window.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: Mediterranean fruit fly, not the Queensland fruit fly of the eastern states, is WA's headline citrus pest. It is established right across the state from Esperance to Carnarvon (though absent from the Ord at Kununurra) and treats citrus as a favoured host, so a backyard pomelo needs baiting and clean picking. Citrus gall wasp is a newer arrival: it is established in most Perth suburbs but has not been found in WA's commercial orchards, which is why home gardeners in country districts are asked to keep a lookout and report it. WA's quarantine keeps the state free of citrus canker and citrus black spot.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: Western Australia keeps tight border quarantine on live plants, and citrus is a known carrier of pests and diseases the state wants to keep out, so propagatable citrus may enter only if it is permitted on the Western Australian Organism List and usually certified and treated first. Many eastern nurseries simply will not post citrus here, and a good deal of the country's pomelo range is sold for the eastern states only, so the in stock table above is filtered to the sellers that can legally ship a pomelo to WA.

faqs.q: Is Perth too cold in winter to grow a pomelo?
faqs.a: No, the Perth coastal plain is warm enough for pomelo, which crops there much as other citrus do. The thing to watch is frost rather than ordinary winter cold: pomelo is the most frost tender of the citrus, so plant it in the warmest, most sheltered, sun facing spot you have, protect a young tree on frosty nights, and keep it off low ground where cold air pools.

faqs.q: Why is it so hard to find a pomelo that ships to Western Australia?
faqs.a: Most of the specialist pomelo varieties are propagated by Queensland and eastern states nurseries, and because citrus carries pests and diseases WA screens for, a lot of that stock is sold for the eastern states only. Live citrus can enter WA only if the variety is permitted under the state's quarantine rules and the plant is certified, so your realistic options are the sellers that already meet those rules, which the table above is filtered to.

NSW.intro: Growing pomelo in New South Wales. Pomelo is a warm climate, frost tender citrus, so in NSW it belongs on the mild subtropical north coast rather than the cold tablelands or the frosty inland.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pomelo needs warmth and hates frost, so its natural home in NSW is the warm, humid, largely frost free far north coast. Down the cooler coast and around Sydney it can still be grown in a warm, sheltered spot, but on the cold tablelands and through the frosty inland it is marginal, and a young tree there needs real frost protection through its first winters. Choose the site for warmth first: a sunny, sheltered, north facing position away from cold air hollows.

sections.heading: Where pomelo grows in NSW
sections.body: For a backyard pomelo the warm Northern Rivers and far north coast, around Lismore, Alstonville and the Tweed, are the pick of the state, sharing the subtropical climate that suits the fruit. The big inland citrus district, the Riverina around Griffith and Leeton, grows the bulk of the state's oranges but has cold, frosty winters that make it a harder home for a frost tender pomelo, so think of pomelo as a coastal and northern tree in NSW rather than an inland one.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: Pomelo ripens from autumn and hangs on the tree into winter, so the warm north coast gives ripe fruit across a long autumn to winter season, a little later than tropical Queensland. As with all pomelo, the fruit does not sweeten further once picked, so taste before you harvest and let it hang until it is genuinely sweet.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Citrus gall wasp, a native insect that began in the south east Queensland and northern NSW region and has since spread right across the state, is the backyard citrus pest most growers meet first, raising woody galls on the stems that are best pruned out in winter. Citrus leafminer silvers and curls the new flush and hits young and potted trees hardest, which matters for a newly planted pomelo. Queensland fruit fly is present and will use citrus as a host in the warmer districts, so net or bait where it occurs.

faqs.q: Will a pomelo fruit on the NSW tablelands or the cold inland?
faqs.a: It is a stretch. Pomelo is the most frost tender of the common citrus, so the cold tablelands and the frosty inland are marginal, and a young tree can be cut back or killed by hard frost. If you are set on trying, grow it in the warmest microclimate you have, against a sun facing wall or in a pot you can move under cover, and accept that it will be slower and lighter cropping than the same tree on the warm coast.

faqs.q: Which pomelo suits the NSW north coast?
faqs.a: The subtropical north coast will ripen almost any pomelo, so pick on flesh and seeds rather than hardiness: red and pink fleshed types such as Carter's Red for sweet eating, or the seedless Nam Roi if you would rather skip the pips. Start with a grafted named tree, give it a warm, sheltered spot, and it should crop reliably in the Northern Rivers warmth.

VIC.intro: Growing pomelo in Victoria. Victoria is cool and frost prone, and pomelo is the most frost tender citrus, so here it is a marginal, warm spot or pot grown tree rather than a reliable garden fruit.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Pomelo is a tropical and subtropical fruit at heart, and Victoria's cool winters and short, mild summers are the opposite of what it wants. It will grow and even fruit in frost free parts of Melbourne, but the quality and the crop are far below what the same tree gives in the subtropics, and frost will damage a young tree outright. Treat pomelo in Victoria as a challenge crop for the warmest, most sheltered corner you have, or grow it in a pot you can shift under cover, and lean on the hardiest pomelo types.

sections.heading: Where pomelo grows in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's citrus is concentrated in the warm, dry north west around Sunraysia and Mildura on the Murray, which has the summer heat citrus needs and grows the bulk of the state's oranges and mandarins. Even there, though, winter frost is the catch for a frost tender pomelo, and the cool central and southern districts, including Melbourne and the Goulburn Valley, are cooler again. Nowhere in Victoria is an easy home for pomelo, so the warmest available microclimate matters more than the district.

sections.heading: Harvest window in VIC
sections.body: Victoria's cool season ripens citrus later than the warm north, so what fruit a Victorian pomelo does set matures late, through autumn and winter, and is best left hanging until it tastes sweet. Expect a lighter, later and less dependable crop than a coastal Queensland or northern NSW tree, which is the price of growing a tropical fruit this far south.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in VIC
sections.body: Citrus gall wasp has spread south over recent decades and is now found right through Victoria, common on suburban Melbourne citrus, so prune the woody galls out in winter before the wasps emerge. Queensland fruit fly has become established in the Sunraysia district, where the long standing Greater Sunraysia pest free area was wound back in 2024, so backyard fruit there now needs fly protection. Frost on young trees, however, is the real limit on a Victorian pomelo, more than any insect.

faqs.q: Can I actually grow a pomelo in Melbourne?
faqs.a: You can grow the tree, but a reliable, good quality crop is hard. Melbourne's cool, short summers and winter frosts are far from what this tropical fruit wants, so even in a frost free pocket the fruit tends to be poorer and lighter than it would be up north. Give it the hottest, most sheltered, sun facing spot you have, protect it from frost, and treat any decent fruit as a win rather than an expectation.

faqs.q: Would a potted pomelo do better than one in the ground in Victoria?
faqs.a: Often, yes. A pot lets you stand a pomelo in the sunniest, most sheltered spot through summer and then move it into a shed, greenhouse or against a warm wall when frost threatens, which is the single biggest hurdle here. Use a tree on a dwarfing rootstock, a large free draining container, and keep the water and feed up, and you give a cool climate pomelo its best chance.
